parts.content:
Write 1 dissimilar SBA question(s) on: 'Children with cerebral palsy undergoing major pelvic surgery experience severe somatic pain and muscle spasm, while systemic opioids can worsen respiratory, gastrointestinal and airway-related complications.'.

Each question should test application of knowledge, not simple recall — a candidate should need to reason from principles rather than just retrieve a fact. Use the retrieve tool to find relevant reference material before writing. Return the result as a QuestionSet.

parts.args: {"query": 'Children with cerebral palsy undergoing major pelvic surgery experience severe somatic pain and muscle spasm, while systemic opioids can worsen respiratory, gastrointestinal and airway-related complications.'}

{"topic":"Final Frca Sample Sbas","exam":"final","curriculum_node_code":null,"model":"openai:gpt-5.5","generated_at":"2026-05-02T00:00:00Z","questions":[{"stem":"A 12-year-old girl with cerebral palsy is scheduled for bilateral pelvic osteotomies. She is gastrostomy-fed, non-ambulant, and takes baclofen and sodium valproate. She has mild scoliosis and occasional gastro-oesophageal reflux.","lead":"Which analgesic strategy is most appropriate to provide effective analgesia while minimising complications specific to her condition?","options":[{"text":"Bilateral fascia iliaca blocks under ultrasound guidance with levobupivacaine 0.25% (0.5 ml/kg per side)","is_correct":false,"explanation":"Fascia iliaca blocks may help anterior hip or thigh pain but do not reliably cover posterior pelvic pain or muscle spasm after pelvic osteotomies. Analgesia would therefore be incomplete."},{"text":"Continuous intravenous morphine infusion titrated to comfort","is_correct":false,"explanation":"Systemic morphine alone is less effective for this severe orthopaedic pain and increases risks of respiratory depression, sedation, ileus and delayed gastric emptying. These are particularly problematic in a child with cerebral palsy, reflux and potentially impaired airway protection."},{"text":"Continuous lumbar epidural with bupivacaine 0.1% and fentanyl 2 micrograms/ml","is_correct":true,"explanation":"A continuous lumbar epidural provides high-quality segmental analgesia for severe pelvic osteotomy pain, reduces muscle spasm and lowers systemic opioid requirements. Low-concentration local anaesthetic with opioid can preserve some motor function while providing prolonged analgesia."},{"text":"Intrathecal morphine (3 micrograms/kg) at the end of surgery combined with multimodal oral analgesia","is_correct":false,"explanation":"Intrathecal morphine may provide early analgesia but has limited and variable duration and carries a risk of delayed respiratory depression. This is undesirable in a child with cerebral palsy and potential respiratory or secretion-management vulnerability."},{"text":"Single-shot caudal epidural with 0.25% bupivacaine (1 ml/kg) and clonidine 1 microgram/kg","is_correct":false,"explanation":"A single-shot caudal would provide insufficient duration for major bilateral pelvic osteotomies, where analgesia is needed for days. Clonidine may also contribute to sedation and hypotension."}],"explanation":"Children with cerebral palsy undergoing major pelvic surgery experience severe somatic pain and muscle spasm, while systemic opioids can worsen respiratory, gastrointestinal and airway-related complications. A continuous lumbar or low thoracic epidural using low-concentration local anaesthetic with opioid provides prolonged segmental analgesia, reduces spasm and minimises opioid exposure. Scoliosis or altered anatomy may make placement more difficult, so placement under direct vision or with appropriate expertise should be considered.","curriculum_node_codes":["2_RA_E_6","2_GA_N_5","2_RA_E_3","2_RA_E_1","2_GA_N_3"],"curriculum_node_scores":[0.714002245630466,0.6564110768649964,0.6560465322083626,0.6318417619161707,0.631785499077516]}]}
